title: 55M with Exertional Chest Pain
summary: A 55-year-old male presents with worsening substernal chest pressure upon exertion.
chief_complaint: Chest pain when walking up stairs.
hpi: Patient reports a 3-week history of episodic substernal chest pressure radiating to his left jaw. The pain occurs after walking roughly two flights of stairs and is relieved by 5 minutes of rest. He denies palpitations, diaphoresis, or syncope. He had a similar episode 6 months ago that he ignored.
family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 60
ros: General: No fever, chills, or weight loss.
Cardiovascular: Positive for exertional chest pain. Negative for palpitations or orthopnea.
Respiratory: Negative for shortness of breath or cough.
GI: Negative for nausea or vomiting.
physical_exam: General: Awake, alert, no acute distress.
CV: RRR, normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.
Resp: CTA bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema, 2+ pulses bilaterally.
labs: Lipid Panel: Total Chol 220, LDL 140, HDL 45, Triglycerides 150
imaging: ECG: Normal sinus rhythm, no acute ST/T wave changes, no Q waves.
Chest X-Ray: Clear lungs, normal cardiac silhouette.
red_flags: Significant family history of premature CAD
teaching_points: Classic presentation of stable angina pectoris involves predictable, exertional chest pain relieved by rest.
Immediate evaluation requires an ECG to rule out ACS and cardiac biomarkers.
Risk factor modification is paramount, especially smoking cessation and tightening glycemic/lipid control.

model_answers.soap_note: S: 55yo male presents with 3 weeks of exertional substernal chest pain radiating to left jaw, relieved by rest. PMH significant for HTN, HLD, T2DM. Social history includes 30 pack-year smoking. O: BP 145/90, HR 82. CV exam normal, no murmurs. Lungs clear. ECG normal sinus rhythm with no acute ischemic changes. Troponin negative. A: Stable angina pectoris with multiple cardiac risk factors. P: Initiate Aspirin 81mg, maximize statin therapy, prescribe PRN sublingual nitroglycerin, refer for outpatient stress testing, counseling on smoking cessation.
model_answers.assessment_and_plan:
1. Stable Angina: Presentation is highly suspicious for CAD. Start ASA 81mg daily and PRN NTG. Order outpatient exercise stress echocardiogram.
2. Hypertension: BP elevated today. Continue Lisinopril, monitor.
3. Hyperlipidemia: LDL elevated. Increase Atorvastatin to 40mg daily.
4. Preventive: Aggressive smoking cessation counseling provided.
